[[ja:%E6%9D%91%E5%9B%BD%E6%B0%8F]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:redirect]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]]
'''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. Their hereditary title ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}) is {{ill|Ren|ja|連}}. Their stronghold was in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}, {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and after {{ill|Murakuni Otokoroe|ja|村国男依}} distinguished himself during the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in {{ill|672|ja|672年}}, they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, but this association led to their decline after the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in {{ill|764|ja|764年}}.
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|702|ja|702年}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲}} ({{ill|707|ja|707年}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|701|ja|701年}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672年}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764年}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.

Their hereditary title ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}) is {{ill|Ren|ja|連}}. Their stronghold was in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}, {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and after {{ill|Murakuni Otokoroe|ja|村国男依}} distinguished himself during the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in {{ill|672|ja|672年}}, they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, but this association led to their decline after the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in {{ill|764|ja|764年}}.
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|702|ja|702年}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲}} ({{ill|707|ja|707年}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|701|ja|701年}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672年}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764年}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.

After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764年}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.

[[ja:%E6%9D%91%E5%9B%BD%E6%B0%8F]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:redirect]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]]
'''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. Their hereditary title ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}) is {{ill|Ren|ja|連}}. Their stronghold was in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}, {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and after {{ill|Murakuni Otokoroe|ja|村国男依}} distinguished himself during the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in {{ill|672|ja|672年}}, they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, but this association led to their decline after the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in {{ill|764|ja|764年}}.
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|702|ja|702年}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲}} ({{ill|707|ja|707年}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|701|ja|701年}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672年}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764年}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)}}

[[ja:%E6%9D%91%E5%9B%BD%E6%B0%8F]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:redirect]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]]
'''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. Their hereditary title ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}) is {{ill|Ren|ja|連}}. Their stronghold was in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}, {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and after {{ill|Murakuni Otokoroe|ja|村国男依}} distinguished himself during the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in {{ill|672|ja|672年}}, they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, but this association led to their decline after the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in {{ill|764|ja|764年}}.
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|702|ja|702年}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲}} ({{ill|707|ja|707年}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|701|ja|701年}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672年}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764年}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.

Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|702|ja|702年}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲}} ({{ill|707|ja|707年}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|701|ja|701年}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672年}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764年}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).

In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764年}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).

[[ja:%E6%9D%91%E5%9B%BD%E6%B0%8F]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:redirect]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] [[Category:Ngoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ngoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]]
'''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. Their hereditary title ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}) is {{ill|Ren|ja|連}}. Their stronghold was in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}, {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and after {{ill|Murakuni Otokoroe|ja|村国男依}} distinguished himself during the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in {{ill|672|ja|672年}}, they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, but this association led to their decline after the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in {{ill|764|ja|764年}}.
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|702|ja|702年}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲}} ({{ill|707|ja|707年}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|701|ja|701年}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672年}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764年}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).

[[ja:%E6%9D%91%E5%9B%BD%E6%B0%8F]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:redirect]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:Shortest 100 pages as of 2025-02-13]] [[Category:Ngoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ngoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]]
'''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. Their hereditary title ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}) is {{ill|Ren|ja|連}}. Their stronghold was in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}, {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and after {{ill|Murakuni Otokoroe|ja|村国男依}} distinguished himself during the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in {{ill|672|ja|672年}}, they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, but this association led to their decline after the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in {{ill|764|ja|764年}}.
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|702|ja|702年}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲}} ({{ill|707|ja|707年}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|701|ja|701年}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672年}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764年}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).

== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|702|ja|702年}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲}} ({{ill|707|ja|707年}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|701|ja|701年}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672年}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764年}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.

[[ja:%E6%9D%91%E5%9B%BD%E6%B0%8F]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:redirect]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:Ngoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:qq]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ngoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]]
'''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}} to the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. Their hereditary title ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ}}) is {{ill|Ren|ja|連}}. Their stronghold was in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}, {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and after {{ill|Murakuni Otokoroe|ja|村国男依}} distinguished himself during the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱}} in {{ill|672|ja|672年}}, they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, but this association led to their decline after the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in {{ill|764|ja|764年}}.
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連}}). It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|702|ja|702年}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|701|ja|701年}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}). In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.

Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).

[[ja:%E6%9D%91%E5%9B%BD%E6%B0%8F]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:kk]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]] [[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:Ngoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Murakuni Clan}}[[Category:qq]] <!--Murakuni Clan--> [[Category:qq]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ngoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Murakuni Clan]][[Category:qq]]

Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764年}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875年}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885年}}).
