== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province }}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|702|ja|702nd year }}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲}} ({{ill|707|ja|707th year }}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝}} ({{ill|701|ja|701st year }}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672nd year }}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764th year }}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875th year }}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885th year }}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.
== Footnotes == {{reflist}}

Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字}} ({{ill|764|ja|764th year }}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875th year }}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885th year }}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.

revision.comment: Bot: Replaced ill templates with appended |12=draft and created corresponding redirect pages.
revision.text:
<!--Murakuni Clan-->
<!--Murakuni Clan-->
'''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏|12=draft}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}} from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}. Their hereditary title ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ|12=draft}}) is {{ill|Ren|ja|連|12=draft}}. Their stronghold was in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|12=draft}}, and after {{ill|Murakuni Otokoroe|ja|村国男依|12=draft}} distinguished himself during the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft}} in {{ill|672|ja|672nd year|12=draft}}, they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂|12=draft}}, but this association led to their decline after the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft}} in {{ill|764|ja|764th year|12=draft}}.
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|12=draft}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造|12=draft}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連|12=draft}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄|12=draft}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡|12=draft}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡|12=draft}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡|12=draft}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川|12=draft}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社|12=draft}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社|12=draft}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)|12=draft}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|12=draft}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝|12=draft}} ({{ill|702|ja|702nd year|12=draft}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲|12=draft}} ({{ill|707|ja|707th year|12=draft}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連|12=draft}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=draft}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝|12=draft}} ({{ill|701|ja|701st year|12=draft}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子|12=draft}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672nd year|12=draft}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫|12=draft}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂|12=draft}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田|12=draft}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=draft}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫|12=draft}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂|12=draft}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|12=draft}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字|12=draft}} ({{ill|764|ja|764th year|12=draft}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾|12=draft}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老|12=draft}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂|12=draft}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介|12=draft}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|12=draft}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関|12=draft}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子|12=draft}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観|12=draft}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875th year|12=draft}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世|12=draft}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=draft}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢|12=draft}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和|12=draft}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885th year|12=draft}}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)|12=draft}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫|12=draft}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.
== Footnotes == {{reflist}}

== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|12=draft}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造|12=draft}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連|12=draft}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄|12=draft}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡|12=draft}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡|12=draft}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡|12=draft}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川|12=draft}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社|12=draft}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社|12=draft}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)|12=draft}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|12=draft}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝|12=draft}} ({{ill|702|ja|702nd year|12=draft}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|12=draft}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲|12=draft}} ({{ill|707|ja|707th year|12=draft}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連|12=draft}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=draft}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝|12=draft}} ({{ill|701|ja|701st year|12=draft}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子|12=draft}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672nd year|12=draft}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫|12=draft}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂|12=draft}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田|12=draft}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=draft}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫|12=draft}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂|12=draft}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|12=draft}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字|12=draft}} ({{ill|764|ja|764th year|12=draft}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾|12=draft}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老|12=draft}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂|12=draft}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介|12=draft}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|12=draft}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関|12=draft}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子|12=draft}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観|12=draft}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875th year|12=draft}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世|12=draft}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=draft}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢|12=draft}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和|12=draft}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885th year|12=draft}}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)|12=draft}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫|12=draft}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.

'''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏|12=draft}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}} from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}. Their hereditary title ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ|12=draft}}) is {{ill|Ren|ja|連|12=draft}}. Their stronghold was in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|12=draft}}, and after {{ill|Murakuni Otokoroe|ja|村国男依|12=draft}} distinguished himself during the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|12=draft}} in {{ill|672|ja|672nd year|12=draft}}, they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂|12=draft}}, but this association led to their decline after the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft}} in {{ill|764|ja|764th year|12=draft}}.

In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=draft}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫|12=draft}} attained the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}}, barely reaching the 5th rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂|12=draft}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|12=draft}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|12=draft}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字|12=draft}} ({{ill|764|ja|764th year|12=draft}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾|12=draft}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老|12=draft}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂|12=draft}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介|12=draft}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|12=draft}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国|12=draft}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関|12=draft}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子|12=draft}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=draft}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観|12=draft}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875th year|12=draft}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世|12=draft}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=draft}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢|12=draft}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和|12=draft}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885th year|12=draft}}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)|12=draft}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫|12=draft}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.
== Footnotes == {{reflist}}

Text replacement - "Tajikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara City)" to "Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)"
18 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:村国氏 up to rev 97572851
revision.text:
'''村国氏'''（むらくにうじ）は、[[日本]]の[[飛鳥時代]]から[[奈良時代]]に地方豪族・中央官人として栄えた一族である。姓（[[カバネ]]）は[[連]]。本拠地は[[美濃国]][[各務郡]]で、[[672年]]の[[壬申の乱]]で[[村国男依]]が活躍してから、中級官人を輩出した。760年代に[[藤原仲麻呂]]に引きたてられたが、そのせいで[[764年]]の[[藤原仲麻呂の乱]]後、衰えた。
== 美濃国における村国氏 == 村国氏は[[美濃国]][[各務郡]]を本拠とした氏族で、[[尾張国造]]（[[尾張氏|尾張連]]）の一族と想定する説がある<ref>太田亮『姓氏家系大辞典』角川書店、1963年</ref>。男依より前の村国氏について直接知りうることはなく、全国的には無名に近い地方小豪族だったと思われる。
[[平安時代]]に作られた『[[和名類聚抄]]』には、美濃国各務郡、[[尾張国]][[葉栗郡]]、[[大和国]][[添下郡]]の三か所に村国郷がある。このうち美濃と尾張の村国郷は、両国の境をなす[[木曽川]]を隔てて向かい合うと推測されているので、もと同じ地域をさしたものであろう。平安時代の「[[延喜式神名帳]]」は、各務郡に[[村国神社]]が2つと[[村国真墨田神社]]があることを記す。葉栗郡には『延喜式』には現れないが別の村国神社があり、村国男依が造ったという伝承を持つ[[音楽寺 (江南市)|音楽寺]]がある。
また、後述のように村国男依とその孫の島主は美濃に縁がある。[[大宝 (日本)|大宝]]2年（[[702年]]）の御野国山方郡三井田里戸籍に村国奥連小竜女なる女がみえる。『[[続日本紀]]』は[[慶雲]]4年（[[707年]]）5月16日に美濃国の村国連等志売が三つ子を産んだと記す。このように村国に関わる事と人は美濃国に集中しており、村国氏を美濃国各務郡と結びつける説は確実性が高い。
『[[日本書紀]]』は[[壬申の乱]]当時の村国男依に[[連]]の姓をつけ、以後の史書もみな村国氏を連姓で記す。しかし、連姓は天武天皇の時代の[[八色の姓]]制定の前後でまったく地位を異にしており、壬申の乱当時における連姓は地方小豪族には高すぎる。『続日本紀』大宝元年（[[701年]]）7月の記事には、乱当時の姓を付して壬申の功臣を並べた中で、村国小依（男依）が姓なしで記されている。これを重視すれば、壬申の乱当時には姓がなく、乱後に連の姓を与えられたと考えることができる。
== 朝廷における村国氏 == 村国氏は、大海人皇子（[[天武天皇]]）に仕えた[[村国男依]]によって『[[日本書紀]]』に登場する。壬申の年（[[672年]]）の6月に、男依は大海人皇子の挙兵命令を美濃国に伝達する使者となった。美濃国出身であることが考慮されたと思われる。7月には主戦線となった近江方面の軍の中心的指揮官として活躍した。男依は[[壬申の乱]]の後、[[封戸]]を与えられた。死後には外[[小紫]]の[[冠位]]を贈られ、子の[[村国志我麻呂]]が[[功田]]を与えられた。最大の功労者に対するものとしては小さく見えるが、地方豪族出身者に対しては大きな優遇であった。
8世紀前半の村国氏は、志我麻呂が[[従五位|従五位上]]、[[村国子虫|子虫]]が[[外位|外]]従五位下を極位とし、[[外位]]ながらかろうじて五位に届く程度の中・下級貴族になった。
[[藤原仲麻呂]]の全盛期には、男依の孫[[村国島主]]が初め仲麻呂に仕えたことから、一族が仲麻呂の引き立てを受けた。[[天平宝字]]8年（[[764年]]）の藤原仲麻呂の乱の直前には、島主が美濃[[掾|少掾]]、[[村国子老]]が[[能登国|能登]][[国司|守]]、[[村国虫麻呂]]が[[越前国|越前]][[介]]であった。この配置は、[[近江国]]を軸に東国と北国の軍事動員をもくろんだ仲麻呂の計画に対応したものと思われる。だが、仲麻呂が軍を興すことに失敗して敗死したため、計画は不発に終わった。島主は[[不破関]]を固めに来た使者に殺され、他の二人も国司の任を解かれた。後に朝廷は村国島主に罪がなかったと認めた。一度は失脚した子老と虫麻呂は以前と同じ位階の官人として復帰を果たした。しかしながら子孫に同じ地位を引き継ぐことはできなかった。
ずっと後に、後宮に勤務した[[村国数子]]が[[貞観 (日本)|貞観]]17年（[[875年]]）に外従五位下になったのが五位に達した最後の村国氏で、その後は[[正六位|正六位上]]の[[村国業世]]とその子[[村国春沢]]の名が『[[日本三代実録]]』[[仁和]]元年（[[885年]]）4月条に見える。
== 参考文献 == * 野村忠夫「村国連男依とその一族」、『岐阜大学研究報告（人文科学）』第4号。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
{{デフォルトソート:むらくにし}} [[Category:氏]] [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:中部地方の氏族]] [[Category:美濃国の氏族]] [[Category:村国氏|*]]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:氏]] to [[Category:Japanese clans]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
Bot: remove [[Category:中部地方の氏族]] from page
revision.text:
'''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏|qq=draft}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft}} from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|qq=draft}} to the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft}}. Their hereditary title ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ|qq=draft}}) is {{ill|Ren|ja|連|qq=draft}}. Their stronghold was in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|qq=draft}}, and after {{ill|Murakuni Otokoroe|ja|村国男依|qq=draft}} distinguished himself during the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft}} in {{ill|672|ja|672nd year|qq=draft}}, they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂|qq=draft}}, but this association led to their decline after the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|qq=draft}} in {{ill|764|ja|764th year|qq=draft}}.
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|qq=draft}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連|qq=draft}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄|qq=draft}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡|qq=draft}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|qq=draft}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡|qq=draft}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡|qq=draft}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川|qq=draft}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社|qq=draft}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社|qq=draft}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)|qq=draft}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|qq=draft}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝|qq=draft}} ({{ill|702|ja|702nd year|qq=draft}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲|qq=draft}} ({{ill|707|ja|707th year|qq=draft}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連|qq=draft}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|qq=draft}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝|qq=draft}} ({{ill|701|ja|701st year|qq=draft}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672nd year|qq=draft}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the Rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫|qq=draft}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂|qq=draft}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田|qq=draft}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the Rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|qq=draft}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫|qq=draft}} attained the Rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft}}, barely reaching the 5th Rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂|qq=draft}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|qq=draft}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|qq=draft}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft}} ({{ill|764|ja|764th year|qq=draft}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾|qq=draft}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老|qq=draft}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|qq=draft}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂|qq=draft}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介|qq=draft}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|qq=draft}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関|qq=draft}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same Rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子|qq=draft}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the Rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観|qq=draft}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875th year|qq=draft}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th Rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世|qq=draft}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|qq=draft}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢|qq=draft}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和|qq=draft}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885th year|qq=draft}}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)|qq=draft}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫|qq=draft}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.
== Footnotes == {{reflist}}

revision.text:
'''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏|qq=draft}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft}} from the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代|qq=draft}} to the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft}}. Their hereditary title ({{ill|Kabane|ja|カバネ|qq=draft}}) is {{ill|Ren|ja|連|qq=draft}}. Their stronghold was in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|qq=draft}}, and after {{ill|Murakuni Otokoroe|ja|村国男依|qq=draft}} distinguished himself during the {{ill|Jinshin War|ja|壬申の乱|qq=draft}} in {{ill|672|ja|672nd year|qq=draft}}, they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂|qq=draft}}, but this association led to their decline after the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|qq=draft}} in {{ill|764|ja|764th year|qq=draft}}.
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in {{ill|Mino Province|ja|美濃国|qq=draft}}’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|qq=draft}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連|qq=draft}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft}} work ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄|qq=draft}}'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡|qq=draft}}, in {{ill|Owari Province|ja|尾張国|qq=draft}}’s {{ill|Haguri District|ja|葉栗郡|qq=draft}}, and in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|qq=draft}}’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡|qq=draft}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the {{ill|Kiso River|ja|木曽川|qq=draft}}, originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社|qq=draft}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社|qq=draft}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)|qq=draft}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|qq=draft}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝|qq=draft}} ({{ill|702|ja|702nd year|qq=draft}}), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀|qq=draft}}'' records that in the 4th year of the {{ill|Keiun era|ja|慶雲|qq=draft}} ({{ill|707|ja|707th year|qq=draft}}) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}'' attributes the surname {{ill|Ren|ja|連|qq=draft}} to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|qq=draft}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the {{ill|Daihō era|ja|大宝|qq=draft}} ({{ill|701|ja|701st year|qq=draft}}) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ({{ill|672|ja|672nd year|qq=draft}}), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the Rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫|qq=draft}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂|qq=draft}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田|qq=draft}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the Rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|qq=draft}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫|qq=draft}} attained the Rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft}}, barely reaching the 5th Rank despite his external status.
At the height of {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂|qq=draft}}’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|qq=draft}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱|qq=draft}} in the 8th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji era|ja|天平宝字|qq=draft}} ({{ill|764|ja|764th year|qq=draft}}), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾|qq=draft}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老|qq=draft}} served as the governor ({{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|qq=draft}}) of {{ill|Noto Province|ja|能登国|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂|qq=draft}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介|qq=draft}}) in {{ill|Echizen Province|ja|越前国|qq=draft}}. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on {{ill|Omi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft}}, but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関|qq=draft}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same Rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子|qq=draft}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the Rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft}} in the 17th year of the {{ill|Jōgan|ja|貞観|qq=draft}} era ({{ill|875|ja|875th year|qq=draft}}); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th Rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世|qq=draft}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|qq=draft}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢|qq=draft}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the {{ill|Ninwa|ja|仁和|qq=draft}} era ({{ill|885|ja|885th year|qq=draft}}).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)|qq=draft}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫|qq=draft}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.
== Footnotes == {{reflist}}

Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same Rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Kazuko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the Rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ju-go-i Ge|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}} in the 17th year of the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan]] era ([[:en:875|875]]); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th Rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Gyōse|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Harusawa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the [[:en:Ninna|Ninwa]] era ([[:en:885|885]]).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|qq=}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nomura Tadao|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Clan|qq=}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] to the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. Their hereditary title ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]) is [[:en:Muraji|Ren]]. Their stronghold was in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagami District|qq=}}, and after [[:en:Murakuni Oyori|Murakuni Otokoroe]] distinguished himself during the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in [[:en:672|672]], they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro|Fujiwara no Nakamaro]], but this association led to their decline after the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in [[:en:764|764]].
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]]’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagami District|qq=}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari Ren|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] work ''[[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagami District, Mino Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]]’s [[:en:Haguri District|Haguri District]], and in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soge District|qq=}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Shrine|qq=}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|qq=}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ongaku-ji (Konan)|qq=}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Shimasu|qq=}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the [[:en:Taiho|Daihō era]] ([[:en:702|702]]), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'' records that in the 4th year of the [[:en:Keiun|Keiun era]] ([[:en:AD 707|707]]) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' attributes the surname [[:en:Muraji|Ren]] to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashoku no Uji|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the [[:en:Taiho|Daihō era]] ([[:en:701|701]]) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天武天皇}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ([[:en:672|672]]), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the Rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshiki|qq=}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Shigamaro|qq=}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōden|qq=}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the Rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ju-go-i Jō|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Komushi|qq=}} attained the Rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ju-go-i Ge|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}, barely reaching the 5th Rank despite his external status.
At the height of [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro|Fujiwara no Nakamaro]]’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Shimasu|qq=}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in the 8th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] ([[:en:764|764]]), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyaku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Koro|qq=}} served as the governor ([[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]]) of [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]], and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Mushi-maro|qq=}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国司}}) in [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on [[:en:Ōmi Province|Omi Province]], but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuwa Barrier|qq=}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same Rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Kazuko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the Rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ju-go-i Ge|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}} in the 17th year of the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan]] era ([[:en:875|875]]); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th Rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Gyōse|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Harusawa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the [[:en:Ninna|Ninwa]] era ([[:en:885|885]]).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|qq=}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nomura Tadao|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.
== Footnotes == {{reflist}}

Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same Rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Kazuko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the Rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ju-go-i Ge|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}} in the 17th year of the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan]] era ([[:en:875|875]]); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th Rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Gyōse|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Harusawa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the [[:en:Ninna|Ninwa]] era ([[:en:885|885]]).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|qq=}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nomura Tadao|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.

[[sn:E6%9D%91%E5%9B%BD%E6%B0%8F]] [[sn:Murakuni Clan]]
[[Category:Clans of Chūbu Region]] [[Category:Murakuni Clan]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:Clans of Mino Province]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:村国氏]] [[Category:paes with no wikidata]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "paes with no wikidata" to "Pages with no Wikidata"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Murakuni Clan''' ({{ill|Murakuni Clan|ja|村国氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Clan|qq=}}, Murakuni-ji) is a family that flourished as regional magnates and central officials in [[:en:Japan|Japan]] from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] to the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. Their hereditary title ([[:en:Kabane|Kabane]]) is [[:en:Muraji|Ren]]. Their stronghold was in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]], {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagami District|qq=}}, and after [[:en:Murakuni Oyori|Murakuni Otokoroe]] distinguished himself during the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] in [[:en:672|672]], they went on to produce mid-level officials. In the 760s they were patronized by [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro|Fujiwara no Nakamaro]], but this association led to their decline after the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in [[:en:764|764]].
== The Murakuni Clan in Mino Province == The Murakuni Clan was based in [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]]’s {{ill|Kagami District|ja|各務郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagami District|qq=}}, and there is a theory that they were a branch of the family of the {{ill|Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|尾張国造|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} ({{ill|Owari Ren|ja|尾張連|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari Ren|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Little is directly known about the Murakuni Clan prior to Otokoroe, and on a national scale they seem to have been an almost obscure minor local clan.
In the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] work ''[[:en:Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', Murakuni communities (郷) are recorded in three locations: in {{ill|Kagami District, Mino Province|ja|美濃国各務郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagami District, Mino Province|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]]’s [[:en:Haguri District|Haguri District]], and in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]’s {{ill|Soge District|ja|添下郡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soge District|qq=}}. It is speculated that the Murakuni-gō in Mino and Owari, which are separated only by the [[:en:Kiso River|Kiso River]], originally referred to the same area. The Heian-era "Engishiki Jinmyōchō" records that there are two Murakuni Shrines ({{ill|Murakuni Shrine|ja|村国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Shrine|qq=}}) and one {{ill|Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|ja|村国真墨田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Mamusuda Shrine|qq=}} in Kagami District. In Haguri District, although no Murakuni Shrine appears in the ''Engishiki'', there is another shrine bearing the name, and the temple {{ill|Ongaku-ji (Konan)|ja|音楽寺 (江南市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ongaku-ji (Konan)|qq=}} carries the tradition of having been built by Murakuni Otokoroe.
Furthermore, as noted later, Otokoroe and his grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Shimasu|qq=}}, had connections with Mino. In the 2nd year of the [[:en:Taiho|Daihō era]] ([[:en:702|702]]), a woman named Murakuni Okuren Kobiryū (奥連小竜女) appears in the family registers of the 【御野国山方郡三井田】. The ''[[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]]'' records that in the 4th year of the [[:en:Keiun|Keiun era]] ([[:en:AD 707|707]]) on May 16, a certain Murakuni Rento-shimē (村国連等志売) of Mino Province bore triplets. In this way, matters and persons associated with the Murakuni Clan are concentrated in Mino Province, lending strong support to the theory linking the Murakuni Clan with Kagami District, Mino.
The ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' attributes the surname [[:en:Muraji|Ren]] to Murakuni Otokoroe at the time of the Jinshin War, and subsequent historical texts consistently record the Murakuni Clan under the Ren surname. However, the Ren surname in the era of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—in the period immediately before and after the establishment of the "Eight Colors of Surnames" ({{ill|Yashoku no Uji|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashoku no Uji|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}})—differed greatly in status, and the Ren surname at the time of the Jinshin War would have been too exalted for a minor local clan. An article in the 1st year of the [[:en:Taiho|Daihō era]] ([[:en:701|701]]) in July, which lists heroes of the Jinshin War along with their surnames, records Murakuni Otokoroe (referred to as Murakuni Koyori (小依)) without any surname. If this is given due weight, it suggests that the clan did not originally possess a surname at the time of the Jinshin War, and that the Ren surname was conferred upon them afterward.
== The Murakuni Clan in the Imperial Court == The Murakuni Clan is introduced in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' through Murakuni Otokoroe, who served {{ill|Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|ja|大海人皇子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天武天皇}} ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). In the year of the Jinshin War ([[:en:672|672]]), in June, Otokoroe was dispatched as an envoy to Mino Province to deliver Prince Ōama's order to raise an army—likely a consideration of his Mino origins. In July, he served as a central commander of the forces in the Omi region, which became the main theater of operations. Following the Jinshin War, Otokoroe was granted a fudo (封戸). After his death, he was awarded the Rank of external {{ill|Koshiki|ja|小紫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshiki|qq=}}, and his son, {{ill|Murakuni Shigamaro|ja|村国志我麻呂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Shigamaro|qq=}}, was granted a honor known as {{ill|Kōden|ja|功田|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōden|qq=}}. Although these honors may seem modest compared to those given to the greatest achievers, they were significant rewards for someone originating from a local magnate.
In the first half of the 8th century, the Murakuni Clan attained mid- to lower-level noble status; Shigamaro reached the Rank of {{ill|Ju-go-i Jō|ja|従五位上|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ju-go-i Jō|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}, while {{ill|Murakuni Komushi|ja|村国子虫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Komushi|qq=}} attained the Rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ju-go-i Ge|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}, barely reaching the 5th Rank despite his external status.
At the height of [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro|Fujiwara no Nakamaro]]’s power, Otokoroe’s grandson, {{ill|Murakuni Shimasu|ja|村国島主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Shimasu|qq=}}, initially entered Nakamaro’s service, and the clan enjoyed his patronage. Just before the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]] in the 8th year of the [[:en:Tenpyō-hōji|Tenpyō-hōji]] ([[:en:764|764]]), Shimasu held the position of {{ill|Shōyaku|ja|少掾|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōyaku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 掾}} in Mino, {{ill|Murakuni Koro|ja|村国子老|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Koro|qq=}} served as the governor ([[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]]) of [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]], and {{ill|Murakuni Mushi-maro|ja|村国虫麻呂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Mushi-maro|qq=}} held the post of secretary ({{ill|Kaisei|ja|介|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国司}}) in [[:en:Echizen Province|Echizen Province]]. This distribution of posts appears to have been part of Nakamaro’s plan to mobilize military forces from eastern and northern regions centered on [[:en:Ōmi Province|Omi Province]], but his failure to raise an army and subsequent defeat meant that the plan never came to fruition. Shimasu was killed by an envoy sent to secure the fortification at {{ill|Fuwa Barrier|ja|不破関|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuwa Barrier|qq=}}, and the other two lost their gubernatorial appointments. Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same Rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Kazuko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the Rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ju-go-i Ge|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}} in the 17th year of the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan]] era ([[:en:875|875]]); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th Rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Gyōse|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Harusawa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the [[:en:Ninna|Ninwa]] era ([[:en:885|885]]).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|qq=}}
== References == * {{ill|Nomura Tadao|ja|野村忠夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nomura Tadao|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, “Murakuni Otokoroe and His Clan,” in ''Gifu University Research Reports (Humanities)'', Issue No. 4.
== Footnotes == {{reflist}}

Later, the Imperial Court exonerated Murakuni Shimasu. Although Koro and Mushi-maro, who had once fallen from power, later returned to serve at the same Rank as before, their descendants were unable to maintain their status.
Much later, a woman named {{ill|Murakuni Kazuko|ja|村国数子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Kazuko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who worked in the Imperial harem, reached the Rank of external {{ill|Ju-go-i Ge|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ju-go-i Ge|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}} in the 17th year of the [[:en:Zhenguan|Jōgan]] era ([[:en:875|875]]); she was the last member of the Murakuni Clan to attain the 5th Rank. Subsequently, the names of {{ill|Murakuni Gyōse|ja|村国業世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Gyōse|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (of the upper {{ill|Shōroku|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}}) and his son {{ill|Murakuni Harusawa|ja|村国春沢|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murakuni Harusawa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} appear in the "Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku" in the 4th clause of the 1st year of the [[:en:Ninna|Ninwa]] era ([[:en:885|885]]).
==See Also== * {{ill|Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|手力雄神社 (各務原市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tejikarao Shrine (Kakamigahara)|qq=}}
